slides.title:
[Talk Title:
The Problem You Solved]
slides.subtitle: [Speaker Name] · [Role] @ [Company]
slides.notes: Title slide. Get to the demo or the core insight within 3 minutes. The title should create curiosity — state the insight or the surprising result, not just the topic.

slides.title: [Why this problem matters in 10 words]
slides.body: [Two to three sentences setting the scene. What does every developer in the room already know about this problem? What have they already tried? End with the thing that still doesn't work.]
slides.notes: Open with what the audience already believes to be true. They should be nodding. Then introduce the twist — the thing that SHOULD work but doesn't. This creates the tension your talk resolves.

slides.title: [Architecture decision in a headline]

items.label: [Component / Layer 1]
items.desc: [What it does and why this choice over the obvious alternative]

items.label: [Component / Layer 2]
items.desc: [The key insight that makes this layer work. What would break without it?]

items.label: [Component / Layer 3]
items.desc: [The integration point or API surface. How does it compose with the rest?]

slides.diagram_hint: [Describe any architecture diagram you want to show: e.g. 'request flow from client → edge → worker → DB']
slides.notes: One architecture diagram is worth 10 bullet slides. Show the system, then explain it top-down or left-to-right. Label every box. For each component, explain WHY this choice, not just WHAT it is.

slides.title: The critical [X] lines
callouts: [Line X: the line that does the surprising thing]
[Line Y: why this is different from the naive approach]
slides.notes: Code slide rules: font size 18pt minimum. No more than 20 lines visible at once. Highlight the 2–3 lines that actually matter with a callout or color. Walk through it slowly — assume the audience is reading as you speak.

slides.title: [X]× improvement / [Y]% reduction in [metric]
slides.notes: Benchmark slides: always show methodology. What was the test setup? What hardware? What load? Results without methodology are marketing. Results WITH methodology are credible. Show the before/after, not just the after.

slides.title: Three pitfalls to avoid

items.label: [Pitfall 1 title]
items.desc: [What goes wrong, why it's non-obvious, and how to detect it before it bites you in production]

items.label: [Pitfall 2 title]
items.desc: [The edge case that only appears at scale. Include the symptoms so the audience can recognize it.]

items.label: [Pitfall 3 title]
items.desc: [The mistake that looks correct in development but fails in production. Include the fix.]

slides.notes: This is often the most memorable slide. Audiences love war stories. Be specific — vague warnings are useless. Name the exact conditions that trigger each pitfall.

slides.title: Three things to remember
items: [Key insight 1 — the mental model shift. One sentence that changes how they think about this problem.]
[Key insight 2 — the practical heuristic. When should they use this? When should they NOT?]
[Key insight 3 — the next step. What to try this week, with a link to a repo or doc.]
slides.notes: End of talk, not end of journey. The takeaways should be actionable by tomorrow morning. If someone only remembers one thing from your talk, which one do you want it to be? Lead with that.

slides.title: Resources
slides.notes: Put this slide up while you answer questions. Have a short URL or QR code. Resources should be immediately actionable — repo first, reading second.

slides.title: Questions?
slides.subtitle: @[handle] · [speaker@email.com]
slides.notes: Q&A slide. Stay in this slide. If there are no questions, return to the most visually interesting slide in your deck to avoid an awkward empty screen.
